### Session 9a6977bf-39a3-4bd2-8d24-32a8fe134b48 - 2026-07-23T05:02:28.239Z

- User asked Claude Code to investigate the origin of phone number (253) 553-6866 that appeared in Jobber analytics with no clear source attribution.
- Claude Code ruled out CallRail tracker assignment (number not in active or disabled list, zero tracked calls), website publication (number absent from got-moles.com source code), and web indexing (no results across search engines).
- Investigation concluded the number is almost certainly Jobber's AI Receptionist dedicated phone line — auto-provisioned when Jobber activates the feature, receives forwarded unanswered calls from the main lines.
- Root cause identified: AI Receptionist sends follow-up texts from its own number; customers save the text thread as "Got Moles" and call it back directly, or it appears in their call history if dialed out — the "leak" is organic customer propagation, not published anywhere.
- Security implication: calls direct to 6866 bypass CallRail entirely (no recording, no source tracking), contributing to the documented 85% missing-attribution problem in the lead-gen funnel.
- Next step: Spencer to log into Jobber via the persistent agentic Chrome window (login screen currently open) using their own credentials; Claude Code will then pull AI Receptionist settings to confirm 6866 assignment and verify what phone number the system reads out to callers.

### Session 9a6977bf-39a3-4bd2-8d24-32a8fe134b48 - 2026-07-23T05:06:36.328Z

- User mentioned discovering they had called (253) 553-6866 off a Google ad the other day without realizing it, which resolved a mystery about call routing.

- Claude explained that (253) 553-6866 is a **Google forwarding number** used for call tracking and conversion reporting, not the actual business number.

- When Google Ads has call reporting enabled, it substitutes its own local forwarding numbers on the ad instead of displaying the real main line (253) 750-0211; calls to the forwarding number get logged as ad conversions, then silently routed to the actual line.

- Ad calls are invisible to CallRail because Google tracks them directly—this explains why CallRail shows GBP, website, Facebook, and Instagram sources but zero Google Ads calls.

- The full call routing map identified: GBPs and website/social go through CallRail tracking numbers → main line; Google Ads goes through Google's 553-6866 → main line (untracked by CallRail); AI answers 750-0211 but Jobber displays a different number (253-326-1740).

- Google Ads conversion counts are slightly inflated by existing customers calling back the forwarding number, which gets counted as a new ad conversion.

- Claude suggested routing ads' call asset to a dedicated CallRail "Google Ads" tracker instead of 750-0211 directly so ad calls are recorded and transcribed like other channels; this would also help with the FAQ mining and 85%-missing-attribution problem from the scale audit.

- A prerequisite blocker identified: the Google Ads API credentials on this install are invalid (`invalid_client`); setting them up is also required for the LSA launch.

- An audit script exists and is ready to run (`_gm-call-asset-audit.mjs`) pending the Ads credentials setup.

### Session 24244cea-410d-490f-a88a-08550f5773f3 - 2026-07-23T05:15:40.108Z

- User asked Claude Code why the task wasn't being completed in the browser instead of delegating to the user
- Claude Code explained three constraints: sign-in requires user credentials and 2FA (which Claude cannot complete), secrets should not pass through Claude's transcript/memory, and the middle steps (OAuth client creation, API enablement) are clickable but relatively cheap for a one-time setup
- Claude Code offered a hybrid approach: user signs into the browser profile once, Claude drives the GCP console for configuration, user pastes credentials into `.env` — reducing user involvement to sign-in plus one paste
- Claude Code concluded with a recommendation for manual completion (10 minutes) for this one-off task as the simplest path, while leaving the hybrid option open if the user preferred to watch Claude click through the console
- The exchange demonstrated deliberate security posture: standing credentials should stay out of transcripts; secrets routing was treated as a hard boundary on this install

- Error indicated the app is in testing phase and only accessible to developer-approved test users
- Claude Code diagnosed the root cause: routereadykits@gmail.com was not added to the app's test-user list in Google Cloud Console
- Provided a specific 3-step fix: navigate to Google Cloud Console auth audience settings → add routereadykits@gmail.com to test users → save
- Explained that project ownership does not automatically grant access; test users must be explicitly listed
- Advised user to confirm completion with "added" so Claude can relaunch the consent flow (which would then succeed)
